headline: Beavers Turn Volcanic Wasteland Into Thriving Wetland
meta_description: After 40 years of volcanic devastation, relocated beavers have transformed a damaged river into a thriving ecosystem—but a new dam project threatens to bury it…
summary_short: Fifty-eight beavers relocated to Washington's North Fork Toutle River have rebuilt a volcano-scarred landscape into a wetland teeming with salmon and wildlife—now at ris…
body:
Forty-three years after Mount St. Helens buried a stretch of the North Fork Toutle River under volcanic sediment, an unlikely restoration team has brought the waterway back to life. Since 2021, 58 beavers have been relocated to the Eco Park Resort near the damaged river, where they've built a complex network of dams and canals that deepened ponds, anchored native willows and alders, and created habitat for Chinook salmon and rainbow trout. The beavers themselves—many rescued from other areas where they faced euthanasia—have become architects of ecological recovery, transforming what Mark Smith, the resort owner, describes as a "volcanic wasteland" into a thriving ecosystem where ducks, elk, and fish now flourish.

Yet this hard-won restoration now faces erasure. The U.S. Army Corps of Engineers recently announced plans to raise a nearby sediment retention structure by 10 feet to manage downstream flood risks. Smith warns that this would trigger a fresh avalanche of sediment, burying the newly formed wetlands and the habitats beavers have so painstakingly rebuilt. After watching his property deteriorate for decades—first from the eruption itself, then from the original dam built in 1989—Smith has filed notice to sue the Corps, advocating instead for nature-based solutions.

The family remains committed despite the setback. At 67, after 35 years on the property, Smith told reporters he's prepared to help the beavers start over if the habitat is destroyed again. His determination reflects a larger lesson: sometimes the most powerful tools for ecological repair aren't engineering projects, but the patient work of wild animals allowed to do what they've evolved to do.
quick_facts: 58 beavers relocated to North Fork Toutle River since 2021
Mount St. Helens eruption in 1980 buried the river in volcanic sediment
Beavers now support Chinook salmon and other native species
Army Corps plans to raise sediment dam 10 feet, threatening restoration
Mark Smith's family owned the property for 35 years
